reasoning:
The exchange rate baseline around March 1, 2026 sits near 1.182. Foreign exchange rates generally follow a random walk, setting a strong base rate around 0.5 for any prediction of an increase. 

For the short term (March 8): We have significant near-term headwinds for the EUR/USD. Recent US economic data, including a stronger-than-expected PPI inflation report and tight jobless claims (199K), point toward persistent US Dollar strength. With the US Non-Farm Payrolls (NFP) report scheduled for March 6, these indicators suggest a resilient US labor market that could delay rate cut expectations, thereby boosting the USD and depressing the EUR/USD pair in the first week. Hence, the probability of an increase by March 8 is placed below 50% at 44%.

For the medium term (March 31): The focus will shift to the March 17-18 FOMC meeting. Markets have recently priced a 60% probability of a Fed rate cut at this meeting. Furthermore, long-term bank consensus overwhelmingly forecasts upward structural drift for the EUR/USD through 2026 (targeting $1.18-$1.24) driven largely by eventual Fed cuts against a holding ECB. This anticipated pivot provides a bullish tailwind in the latter half of March, offsetting the early-month USD strength. Consequently, a recovery is plausible. Given the competing forces—resolute near-term data vs. structural rate-cut expectations—and the longer forecasting horizon, uncertainty is higher. Thus, the estimate moves closer to 50%, set marginally positive at 52%.
